cards.text:
School Vacations at Ajji's
Bhoomi and Surya enjoy visiting their grandmother, Ajji, in a village near the Western Ghats forest during vacations.

The Serene Environment
The village has fresh and cool air, surrounded by hills, streams, and diverse flora and fauna.

The Treasures of Nature
  • The place is rich in natural resources like pure air, fertile soil, and abundant sunlight.

Nature's Role
Trees provide food and shelter for animals, birds, and insects.

Water's Vital Use
Bhoomi acknowledges water's necessity for drinking and agriculture.

Ajji's Teachings on Air
Ajji describes the importance of air for survival and comfort.

Breathing Exercises
Deep breathing helps fill the lungs with fresh air, promoting health.

Oxygen's Essential Role
  • Oxygen is crucial for bodily functions.
  • Living beings require it for survival.

Composition of Air
  • Mainly consists of nitrogen (78%), oxygen (21%), and small amounts of argon and carbon dioxide.

Definition of Wind
Moving air is termed as wind, which can vary from gentle breezes to powerful storms.

Noticing Wind's Effects
  • Wind causes trees to rustle and clothes to sway.

Paper Pinwheel - Firki
A firki rotates when air is blown due to wind action.

Windmills: Harnessing Wind
  • Windmills work similarly to firkis and can generate electricity.

Windmill Farms in India
  • Muppandal in Tamil Nadu, Jaisalmer in Rajasthan are notable farms.

Air and Water: Essentials
Both air and water are vital for human survival and wellbeing.

Water covers about two-thirds of the Earth’s surface. Most of the water is found in oceans and seas. However, this water is saline or salty. This saline water is not fit for domestic, agricultural and industrial use.
Water harvesting is one of the methods for conserving water. In many buildings, rainwater is collected and stored in large quantities for later use. This is called rainwater harvesting.

Bhoomi and Surya's Activities
Bhoomi and Surya assist their grandmother, Ajji, in filling water troughs for cows and watering garden plants.

Watering Techniques
Ajji teaches Bhoomi and Surya efficient watering techniques to ensure no water is wasted.

Earth's Water Distribution
Two-thirds of the Earth's surface is covered in water, mostly saline, found in oceans and seas.

Freshwater Sources
Freshwater is found in ice sheets, snow, rivers, lakes, and underground, but only a small portion is easily accessible.

Importance of Saving Water
Water conservation is crucial as freshwater sources are limited and needed for various daily activities.

Reducing Water Wastage
  • Turn off taps when not in use.
  • Fix water leaks promptly.
  • Recycle water.
  • Implement water harvesting.

Water Pollution Sources
Throwing waste in water bodies and industrial waste dumped into rivers are major sources of water pollution.

Discussing Water Pollution
It's vital to identify and discuss methods to reduce water pollution and promote clean water usage.

Water Shortage in India
Many regions face water scarcity, leading people to travel long distances for drinking water.

Water Access Inequality
Access to water varies widely, highlighting the need for conservation and equitable distribution.

Rainwater Harvesting
Collecting and storing rainwater for future use is an effective water conservation method.

Traditional Water Harvesting
Stepwells, like Bawadis and Vavs, are traditional methods used in water-scarce regions for water storage.

Significance of World Water Day
World Water Day, observed on March 22, underscores the importance of water conservation and resource management.

The Water Cycle
The Sun is crucial in the water cycle by driving the evaporation process.

Research Local Practices
Investigating traditional water harvesting in local areas can offer insights into sustainable practices.

Sunny Day Activities
Bhoomi and Surya assist Ajji in drying chillies under the sun, utilizing solar heat as a natural drying agent.

Sun: A Source of Energy
The Sun is the primary energy provider on Earth, assisting both plant and animal life.

Using Dried Chillies
Dried chillies, preserved under the sun's heat, are used when fresh chillies are unavailable.

Heat and Light Uses
The Sun's heat and light serve various purposes in daily life, from drying foods to providing warmth.

Bhoomi's Illustrations
Bhoomi creates pictures depicting the Sun's applications, encouraging others to identify additional examples.

Field Observation
While passing by a field, Bhoomi and Surya discuss the Sun's crucial role in energy distribution on Earth.

Photosynthesis Process
Sunlight allows plants to create food via photosynthesis, a process vital for life.

Energy for All Living Beings
Sunlight offers necessary heat and light to living organisms, forming a foundation for life.

Morning Rituals
In India, many households thank the Sun through early morning rituals, recognizing its life-giving energy.

Solar Energy Harvest
Solar panels transform the Sun's rays into electricity, illustrating solar energy conversion.

Solar Cooking and Heating
  • Solar cookers utilize the Sun's rays for cooking.
  • Solar water heaters use solar energy for heating water.

Life Dependency
Life on Earth intimately depends on the Sun's energy, supporting food production and growth.

Energy Cycle on Earth
The continuous energy cycle is sustained by the Sun, linking plants, animals, and humans.

Illustrating Solar Benefits
Khoomi's diagrams endeavor to illustrate several solar energy advantages.

Global Dependency on the Sun
Globally, life forms are reliant on solar energy for sustenance and growth.
Forests Definition Forests are large areas with dense growth of various types of plants.
Impact of Forests - Forests provide food and shelter to many wild animals, including birds and insects.
Animal Dependency in Forests Every animal depends on other life forms for survival, ensuring a diverse ecosystem.
Decreasing Forest Cover Human activities, such as large-scale cutting of trees, have reduced forest cover.
Time to Replenish New forests or restoring lost forests takes years, necessitating responsible forest use.
Van Mahotsav Event A weeklong forest festival celebrated in July, focusing on planting trees and raising awareness.
Tradition of Respecting Forests India has a long tradition of respecting, protecting, and preserving forests.
Sacred Groves Forests that are considered sacred and protected as part of India's tradition.
Chipko Movement - Originated in the early 1970s in Uttarakhand. - Local women hugged trees to prevent them from being felled.
Soil Conservation by Forests - Plant roots hold soil, preventing erosion. - Fallen leaves decay, enriching soil with nutrients.
Role of Decayed Leaves Decayed leaves provide nutrients to the soil, aiding the growth of new plants and trees.
Natural Recycling Forests demonstrate recycling in nature by enriching soil and supporting plant growth.
Respecting Forest Fruits Tradition in some villages to leave forest fruits for animals and birds rather than plucking them.
Conservation Awareness Van Mahotsav aims to foster awareness about the importance of forest conservation.
Community Participation Creating local awareness and organizing events like Van Mahotsav in communities promotes forest conservation.

Returning from the Forest
Bhoomi, Surya, and Ajji return home from their forest visit.

Soil Preparation for Planting
Bhoomi and Surya assist Ajji in preparing the garden soil for planting vegetables.

Preparing the Soil
Ajji advises gently digging the soil to loosen the lumps, aiding root growth and air space.

Observations in Soil
The soil reveals small pebbles, plant roots, and earthworms.

Activity 11.4: Soil Investigation
Always wash hands after handling soil, as it may contain harmful germs.

Contents of Soil
  • Sand
  • Insects and worms
  • Microorganisms

Formation of Soil
Soil is formed by the weathering of rocks due to Sun, water, and living organisms over thousands of years.

Types and Uses of Soil
Different soils support biodiversity and are useful for growing plants or making construction materials like bricks.

Rocks in Construction
  • Used for houses, buildings, roads, etc.
  • Slate for roofing
  • Laterite as building material

Significance of Rocks
Rocks have been used for making tools like hand axes and arrowheads.

Minerals in Rocks
Rocks consist of minerals, which are sources of metals like aluminium, gold, and copper.

Minerals in Manufacturing
Minerals are essential in producing vehicles, electronics, and jewelry.

Mining and Minerals in Technology
Minerals are crucial in basic mobile phone production, using gold, silver, copper, and more.

Rocks and Minerals and Environmental Consideration
Rocks take millions of years to form, necessitating their conservation.

Transportation of Rocks and Minerals
Vehicles using fossil fuels enable the transportation of rocks and minerals.
Petrol, diesel and kerosene are obtained from petroleum. Petroleum along with natural gas and coal are commonly called fossil fuels.
Natural gas is used for cooking and generating electricity. Nowadays, it is also used in the form of Compressed Natural Gas (CNG) as a fuel for vehicles. It is a cleaner fuel than petrol or diesel.
Fuels for Vehicles Petrol and diesel are the two most commonly used fuels for vehicles.
Origin of Fuels Petrol, diesel, and kerosene are obtained from petroleum.
Classification of Fossil Fuels Petroleum, natural gas, and coal are termed as fossil fuels.
Formation of Fossil Fuels These fuels are formed from micro-organisms and plant remains buried deep inside the earth over millions of years.
Use of Natural Gas Natural gas is used for cooking and generating electricity. It is also utilized as Compressed Natural Gas (CNG) for vehicles, which is cleaner than petrol or diesel.
Coal's Primary Use Coal is primarily used for electricity generation and is found in multiple parts of India.
Importance of India's Coal - Major coal-producing states in India should be identified and marked on a map. - Coal's significance in India's energy sector is noteworthy.
Traditional Cooking Fuels Previously, coal, wood, and dung cakes were commonly used as cooking fuels.
Transition to Modern Cooking Fuels Natural gas and Liquefied Petroleum Gas (LPG) have replaced these traditional fuels due to lesser pollution.
Fossil Fuel Availability Fossil fuels exist in limited quantities and will deplete with continuous, unregulated use.
Environmental Impact of Fossil Fuels Burning fossil fuels releases smoke and carbon dioxide, polluting the air.
Pollution due to Fossil Fuels Excessive reliance on fossil fuels for transportation and domestic use has led to significant air pollution.
Need for Alternative Energy Exploring alternative energy sources is crucial to prevent the rapid depletion of fossil fuels.
Air Pollution Concerns The burning of fossil fuels contributes to large-scale air pollution.
Conservation of Fossil Fuels Responsibly managing the use of fossil fuels is essential to ensure sustainable environmental practices.
Heat and light from the Sun, water from rivers, and food from plants and animals. These resources which we get from nature are called natural resources.
Electric bulbs, furniture, solar panels, bicycles, etc., make our lives comfortable. All such resources created by human beings are called humanmade resources.
Resources which get renewed, replenished or restored within a reasonable period of time are called renewable resources. Air, water and forest are some of the examples of renewable natural resources.

Fossil fuels take millions of years to form. They are found in limited quantities and once used, they get exhausted. They are not produced or replenished within a reasonable period of time. These resources are called non-renewable resources. Examples of non-renewable natural resources are minerals, soil, rocks, coal, petroleum and natural gas.

Definition of Natural Resources
Natural resources are resources that are derived from nature and are essential for sustaining life on Earth.

Examples of Natural Resources
  • Air: Necessary for breathing in all living organisms.
  • Water: Obtained from rivers and used for drinking, agriculture, etc.
  • Sun: Source of heat and light energy.
  • Forests: Provide oxygen, habitat, and resources like wood.
  • Soil: Supports plant life and agriculture.
  • Rocks and Minerals: Used in construction and manufacturing.
  • Fossil Fuels: Used for energy, such as coal, petroleum, and natural gas.

Human-Made Resources
Human-made resources are items created by humans to enhance comfort and convenience, like electric bulbs, furniture, solar panels, and bicycles.

Renewable Resources
Resources that are naturally replenished over time through natural processes.
  • Examples: Air, Water, Forests.
  • Renewable nature ensures a sustainable supply when used judiciously.

Non-Renewable Resources
Resources that are available in limited quantities and cannot be replenished within a reasonable timeframe.
  • Examples: Fossil fuels like coal, petroleum, and minerals.
  • These resources take millions of years to form and get exhausted once used.

Sustainable Use of Resources
Using resources in a manner that ensures availability for future generations.
  • Collect only naturally fallen fruits to leave enough for other creatures.

Role of Renewable Resources
Renewable resources play a crucial role in meeting the long-term needs of the planet. Nature naturally renews these resources.

Sun as a Natural Resource
The Sun is a constant source of light and heat, influencing various natural processes on Earth.

Difference Between Renewable and Non-Renewable Resources
  • Renewable resources can replenish in a short period; non-renewable cannot.
  • Examples: Water vs. Oil.

Plants and Animals
Provide food resources crucial for the survival of life on Earth. They form part of the natural resource pool.

Practical Use of Natural Resources
  • Natural resources serve as raw materials for creating human-made resources.
  • Examples: Wood from trees is used in furniture making.

Impact of Overusing Non-Renewable Resources
Overuse leads to depletion and potential scarcity, causing environmental and economic issues.

Importance of Forests
Forests contribute to oxygen production, carbon sequestration, and supporting biodiversity.

Renewable Resource Restoration Example
Seeds carried by animals and birds help in growing new trees, showcasing natural resource regeneration.

Environmental Stewardship
Active protection and responsible management of natural resources ensure ecological balance and sustainability.
Bhoomi and Surya's Holiday Bhoomi and Surya spent a delightful holiday at their Ajji's house.
Amma's Arrival Amma arrives to pick Bhoomi and Surya up after their holiday.
Gifts to Take Home They received dried chillies and noticed vegetable plants growing in Ajji's garden.
City Observations Upon reaching the city, Bhoomi and Surya observed the color changes in the skyline.
Air Quality in the City The air in the city was noticeably less fresh due to fewer trees and vehicle emissions.
Vehicle Pollution Explanation Amma explained how fossil fuels used in vehicles create smoke and air pollution.
Efforts to Reduce Pollution There are now vehicles, like electric ones, that cause less pollution.
Everyday Natural Resources We daily use resources like air, water, soil, and food from plants and animals.
Importance of Conservation We should conserve natural resources to meet present needs without depleting them for the future.
Gandhi's Wisdom Earth provides enough for everyone's needs but not everyone's greed.
Definition of Resources Resources required for survival are nature-provided and known as natural resources.

Types of Natural Resources
  • Air
  • Water
  • Energy from Sun
  • Forests
  • Soil
  • Rocks, minerals, fossil fuels
Human-made Resources Resources created by humans to meet their needs are termed human-made resources.

Renewable vs Non-renewable Resources
  • Renewable resources can be replenished naturally.
  • Non-renewable resources are limited and cannot be replenished quickly.
Responsible Resource Usage All living beings depend on natural resources, and mindful usage is vital.
Resources required for our survival are provided by nature.
Resources provided by nature are called natural resources.
Some important natural resources are air, water, energy from the Sun, forests, soil, rocks, minerals and fossil fuels.
All living beings, including humans, depend on natural resources for their survival so we should use them judiciously.
